summary: Drew DeVault argues that GitHub's recommended 'stale bot', which automatically closes issues after 60 days of inactivity, is actively harmful to open source projects. He contends that maintainers have no obligation to respond to every issue and that open issues serve as valuable collaborative spaces where users can accumulate details, workarounds, and eventually contribute fixes. Having many open issues signals popularity, not neglect. He contrasts GitHub's approach with SourceHut's mailing-list-first workflow, where discussion refines bug reports before they become tickets, resulting in higher-quality, actionable items.
key_points: The stale bot destroys valuable collaborative spaces where users accumulate bug details and workarounds over time
Maintainers are not obligated to respond to, fix, or even acknowledge every issue — and shouldn't feel shame about open issues
Issue trackers should be viewed as community workspaces, not personal to-do lists for maintainers
A mailing-list-first workflow (as SourceHut uses) filters and refines reports before they become tickets, improving quality and eliminating duplicates
money_quotes: This is a terrible, horrible, no good, very bad idea.
You are not obligated to respond to every issue, implement every feature request, or fix every bug, or even acknowledge them in any way.
It does not matter if this process takes days or weeks or years to complete.
It's entirely valid to just ignore your bug tracker entirely and leave it up to users to deal with themselves.
There is no shame in having a lot of open issues — if anything, it signals popularity.
Don't deny your users access to an important mutual support resource, and a crucial funnel to bring new contributors into your project.
key_insight: Issue trackers are communal knowledge bases and contributor funnels, not maintainer to-do lists, and automatically closing them destroys long-term collaborative value.
